elements.sent-2411-003: Rodrigues {Bifurcations and canards in the FitzHugh-Nagumo system: a case study system: a tutorial in fast-slow dynamics alves o e Decis\~{a o Econ\'omica Rua do Quelhas 6, Lisboa 1200-781, Portugal arodrigues@iseg.ulisboa.pt.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: In this article, we study the FitzHugh-Nagumo $(1,1)$--fast-slow system where the vector fields associated to the slow/fast equations come from the reduction of the Hodgin-Huxley model for the nerve impulse.
elements.sent-2411-005: After deriving dynamical properties of the singular and regular cases, we perform a bifurcation analysis and we investigate how the parameters (of the affine slow equation) impact the dynamics of the system.
elements.sent-2411-006: The study of codimension one bifurcations and the numerical locus of canards concludes this case-study.
elements.sent-2411-007: All theoretical results are numerically illustrated.
elements.sent-2411-008: Many animals have cells in their various physiological systems, such as the nervous, muscular, and cardiac systems, that are sensitive to certain electrical stimuli.
elements.sent-2411-009: These cells remain at rest most of the time, react to electrical stimulation at a given moment, and then return to a resting state until they are stimulated again.
elements.sent-2411-010: This oscillatory and excitability dynamics is present in neuronal activity and cardiac rhythm -- see for instance Keener \& Sneyd \citet{keener .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In 1952, Hodgkin \& Huxley \citeyear{Hodgkin5 developed a mathematical model that described the propagation of electrical signals along a squid's giant axon, known as the Hodgkin-Huxley (HH) model.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The authors related the excitability of squid nerve cells and the resulting electrical impulse to the potential difference between the inside and outside of the squid's axon membrane arising from the movement of sodium and potassium ions across the membrane.
elements.sent-2411-013: Using experimental data, they established the propagation of electrical signals along the squid's giant axon through a nonlinear system of four differential equations \cite{Hodgkin5 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In 1960--1961 Richard FitzHugh \cite{Fitzhugh1,Fitzhugh2 created a simplified version of the Hodgkin-Huxley model, considering only two variables.}
elements.sent-2411-015: His goal was to simplify the HH model in order to make the dynamics of excitability more perceptible.
elements.sent-2411-016: The system developed by FitzHugh in 1961 has the following form: \begin{equation}\label{fitz} \begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{dt} = a\left(x - \frac{x^3}{3} - y + I\right) \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1}{a}\left(x - by - c\right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $a\in \RR\backslash \{0\}, b,c\in \RR$ are constants and $I$ may be seen as the intensity of a stimulus applied to the axon membrane.
elements.sent-2411-017: Variables $x$ and $y$ match with the pairs of variables $\left(v,m\right)$ and $\left(h,n\right)$ from the HH model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-018: During the 1960s, Jin-Ichi Nagumo constructed an electrical circuit that recreated FitzHugh's system \cite{Nagumo .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Nowadays, due to the contributions of both researchers, this model is known as the FitzHugh-Nagumo (FH-N) model.
elements.sent-2411-020: This model has been extensively studied see, for instance, \citet{lacasa24 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-021: By changing the time scale of system \eqref{fitz , considering $\tau=t/ a$ and $\varepsilon=1/a^2$, we obtain an equivalent version of the FH-N model as follows: \begin{equation}\label{epsfitz} \begin{aligned} \varepsilon\frac{dx}{d\tau} = x - \frac{x^3}{3} - y + I \frac{dy}{d\tau} = x - by - c. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For $0<\varepsilon\ll1$, or equivalently, $a\gg1$, system \eqref{epsfitz is what we call a fast-slow system.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{epsfitz}  \varepsilon\frac{dx}{d\tau} = x - \frac{x^3}{3} - y + I \frac{dy}{d\tau} = x - by - c. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-022: A fast-slow system is a system of differential equations in which some variables have their derivatives with larger magnitude than others.
elements.sent-2411-023: This leads to a system with multiple time scales.
elements.sent-2411-024: The general approach to this type of systems starts by grouping the variables in two disjoint sets: fast variables and slow variables.
elements.sent-2411-025: This separation is introduced in system \eqref{epsfitz by the parameter $\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In Doss-Bachelet {\em et al. \citet{artBachelet the authors notice that the FH-N model presents two distinct dynamics near a Hopf bifurcation: an attracting focus where all trajectories converge to it if the parameter is less than the bifurcation value; and an attracting relaxation-oscillation periodic solution (limit cycle) if the parameter is greater than the bifurcation point.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The work of \citeauthor{artBachelet proceeds with the analysis of the dynamics and applies it to generate bursting oscillations from two coupled FH-N systems, thus leaving the FH-N model little explored.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Our goal with the present article is to exhibit the various dynamics in the multiple time scale model and motivate their existence in the light of methods from geometric singular perturbation theory and bifurcation theory.
elements.sent-2411-029: This article is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section \ref{sec:sing we introduce general concepts of fast-slow systems and geometric singular perturbation theory and apply them to the analysis of the singular case of the FH-N system.}
elements.sent-2411-031: A relaxation-oscillation periodic solution is studied with an estimate of its period in Section \ref{sec:per .}
elements.sent-2411-032: In Section \ref{sec:reg we describe Fenichel's theorem and link singular to regular dynamics and the way the latter converges to the former as $\varepsilon\to0$ applying it to FH-N.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In Section \ref{sec:bif we perform a bifurcation analysis of FH-N and investigate the influence of parameters in the qualitatively different dynamics of the model.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The study of singularities and the emergence of canard trajectories in Section \ref{sec:canards concludes this tutorial.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Section \ref{discussion discusses the results presented and states the natural continuation of the present work.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We have endeavoured to make a self contained exposition bringing together all topics related to the proofs.
elements.sent-2411-037: We have drawn illustrative figures to make the paper easily readable.
elements.sent-2411-038: All figures in this article were created through numerical simulations conducted in Matlab, using integration functions such as ode15s or ode23s, and, in more sensitive examples, the MatCont toolbox developed by Willy Govaerts, Yuri A.
elements.section-2411-002: Multiple Time Scale Theory and the FitzHugh-Nagumo
elements.sent-2411-040: System In this section, general concepts of two time scale systems are introduced and illustrated with the FitzHugh-Nagumo system.
elements.sent-2411-041: Further results on fast-slow systems may be found in \citet{kuehn .}
elements.sent-2411-042: We start by introducing some necessary definitions.
elements.sent-2411-043: For $m, n\in \NN$, a $(m,n)$--fast-slow system is a system of $m + n$ ordinary differential equations of the form: \begin{equation}\label{deflento} \begin{aligned} \varepsilon\,\dfrac{\mathrm{d}x}{\mathrm{d}\tau}=\varepsilon\Dot{x}=f(x,y,\varepsilon), \phantom{\varepsilon\,}\dfrac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}\tau}=\phantom{\varepsilon}\Dot{y}=g(x,y,\varepsilon), \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: Considering $t=\tau/\varepsilon$, system \eqref{deflento is equivalent to: \begin{equation}\label{defrapido} \begin{aligned} \dfrac{\mathrm{d}x}{\mathrm{d}t}=x'=\phantom{\varepsilon\,} f(x,y,\varepsilon), \dfrac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}t}=y'=\varepsilon\, g(x,y,\varepsilon). \end{aligned}\end{equation} The fast time scale is represented by $t$ while $\tau$ refers to the slow time scale.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In order to better understand the concepts and the terminology associated with these systems, the following definitions and results are illustrated by the FitzHugh-Nagumo (1,1)--fast-slow system.
elements.sent-2411-046: As described in \citet{artBachelet , consider the FitzHugh-Nagumo (1,1)--fast-slow system: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{fhn} \text{Slow time scale } \left(\tau\right) \qquad\qquad \text{Fast time scale } {\textstyle\left(t=\tau/\varepsilon\right)} \varepsilon\,\dfrac{\mathrm{d}x}{\mathrm{d}\tau}=\varepsilon\Dot{x}=f(x,y,\varepsilon) \qquad\qquad \dfrac{\mathrm{d}x}{\mathrm{d}t}=x'=\phantom{\varepsilon}\,f(x,y,\varepsilon) \phantom{\varepsilon\,}\dfrac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}\tau}=\phantom{\varepsilon}\Dot{y}=g(x,y,\varepsilon) \qquad\qquad \dfrac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}t}=y'=\varepsilon\,g(x,y,\varepsilon), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $f,g:\; \RR^3\longrightarrow\RR$ are given by \begin{equation}f(x,y,\varepsilon)=-y+4x-x^3, \quad g(x,y,\varepsilon)=x-by-c\end{equation} with parameters $b,c \in{\RR}$ and $0<\varepsilon\ll1$.. $\diamond$.}

elements.sent-2411-050: For $\varepsilon=0$, system \eqref{deflento in the slow time scale becomes a system of differential-algebraic equations: \begin{equation}\label{deffaselenta} \begin{aligned} 0=f(x,y,\varepsilon), \Dot{y}=g(x,y,\varepsilon). \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-285: This article studies the dynamics of a fast-slow system derived from the FitzHugh-Nagumo model according to the geometric singular perturbation theory and the bifurcation theory methods.
elements.sent-2411-286: In particular, we provide an analytic proof that the fast-slow FH-N system presents relaxation oscillation dynamics as well as periodic solutions induced by Hopf bifurcation.
elements.sent-2411-287: We emphasise the emergence of a homoclinic orbit and canards connecting these two distinct dynamics.

elements.sent-2411-308: In 1952, Hodgkin \& Huxley \citeyear{Hodgkin5 developed a mathematical model that described the propagation of electrical signals along a squid's giant axon, known as the {\em Hodgkin-Huxley (HH) model.}}
elements.sent-2411-309: In 1960--1961 Richard FitzHugh \cite{FitzHugh1,FitzHugh2 created a simplified version of the HH model, considering only two variables.}
elements.sent-2411-310: The system developed by by FitzHugh in 1961 has the following form: \begin{equation}\label{Fitz} \begin{aligned} \frac{dx}{dt} = a\left(x - \frac{x^3}{3} - y + I\right) \frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{1}{a}\left(x - by - c\right), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $a\in \RR\backslash \{0\}, b,c\in \RR$ are constants and $I$ may be seen as the intensity of a stimulus applied to the axon membrane.
elements.sent-2411-311: Variables $x$ and $y$ of \eqref{Fitz describe the fast evolution of the membrane voltage of a neuron as well as the activation of sodium channels, and the inactivation/activation of the sodium/potassium channels, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-312: They match with the pairs of variables $\left(v,m\right)$ and $\left(h,n\right)$ from the HH model, respectively -- cf.. \cite{FitzHugh2,keener .}
elements.sent-2411-313: During the sixties, Jin-Ichi Nagumo constructed an electrical circuit that recreated FitzHugh's system \cite{Nagumo .}
elements.sent-2411-314: By changing the time scale of system \eqref{Fitz , considering $\tau=t/ a$ and $\varepsilon=1/a^2$, we obtain an equivalent version of the FH-N model as follows: \begin{equation}\label{epsFitz} \begin{aligned} \varepsilon\frac{dx}{d\tau} = x - \frac{x^3}{3} - y + I \frac{dy}{d\tau} = x - by - c. \end{aligned}\end{equation} For $0<\varepsilon\ll1$, or equivalently, $a\gg1$, system \eqref{epsFitz is what we call a fast-slow system.}}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Novelty and structure of the article
elements.sent-2411-344: This article studies the dynamics of a fast-slow system derived from the FH-N model according to the geometric singular perturbation theory and the bifurcation theory methods.
elements.sent-2411-345: We provide an analytic proof that the fast-slow FH-N system presents relaxation oscillation dynamics as well as periodic solutions induced by Hopf bifurcation -- we fully analyse the type of bifurcations and we check rigorously all conditions for the emergence of a homoclinic orbit and canards connecting these two distinct dynamics.
